paragraphs.text: A burial register might seem a strange place to get a thrill. But death record no. 1183, in an aged burial register at

, is not just an ordinary tombstone write-up, beginning simply with a name, continuing with two dates and closing with a "requiescat." Behind entry 1183 is a story thrilling as an epic; a love "strong as death;" a strange incident haunting the shadowy region which separates fact from fiction.
The story is almost unbelievable. It sounds like something from between the covers of a fairy tale book.
who played the major roles in its climax, it came in time to be looked upon by some as a good yarn -- such as only men of the sea can conjure up -- and nothing more.
. The only extant authentic accounts of her strange adventure are those of

") whose narratives have all the fresh qualities of being those of eye-witnesses. The strange tale needs no romantic back-drops or highly dramatic embellishments. In itself it contains all the elements of tragedy, romance, nobility, courage and even a touch of the humorous.

came from is not known, but writers (with what proof it is difficult to say), speak of
being of a superior physical make-up; the women being tall, light-complexioned and handsome. Remains and relics indicate
origin, and this would account for Juana Maria's fair features and pleasing manners.
the numerous population of this island
dwindled to the meager 20 or so

went under the not too complimentary name of "Peor es Nada." Depending on the degree of excellence you care to attribute to

, you may translate her name: "better than nothing," "worse than nothing," or "there is nothing worse." There is no eye-witness account of the exact nature of events that followed upon the arrival of the "

told him it happened in the following manner. They were to remove

made signs that she was going back to her rancheria for
who had been overlooked in the confusion. While
, put off from shore and ran before the wind.
This simple account of the abandoning of the unfortunate
has been variously amplified -- but with what basis it is difficult to determine.
, writing in the September 1880 edition of Scribner's Monthly, depicts the storm as already reaching a violent stage before
even landed. In the consequent confusion and excitement in evacuating
was missing until she was already aboard the "
, but to no avail. She became desperate, and jumping overboard struck out through "the kelpy waters for the shore
." No one made any attempt to save her, and "in a moment she was lost in the seething waves" as the "
staggered through the waves." The magazine
, in its Oct. edition of 1893, carried an article by
remarks that from the reports handed down we can infer that
made signs to her that they would be back "manana." "She put her head on the ground and laid on the ground and cried; and they never came back." And then
, in an article entitled, "The Tragedy of San Nicholas Island," published in
as having two children. She writes that as the last boat was to push off from shore, "
in her arms sprang on shore.

, a child of 8 years, had slipped from her side in the confusion -- gone possibly in search of some remembered trinket dear to her childish heart -- or perhaps overcome with terror at the unusual migration of
." And thus has immagination added its bit to the story.
Returning to the Nidever account (the only reliable one of the lot), we find that
, which was in popular demand, was ordered immediately to
to carry a load of lumber to
capsized and washed out to sea.
There was now no other vessel on
that was fit to attempt the trip to
, and destined her to a life that was to make that of Robinson Crusoe look like a picnic in comparison.
was a far cry from any romantic environ of
, and could by no stretch of the imagination be termed a little bit of heaven that fell out of the blue one day.
Though calm and serene at certain seasons, the surrounding waters were not so throughout the entire year.
A good idea of the climatic conditions of
, Ph. D., who wrote in the

in October 1889, and several times during his stay saw the sea lashed into fury, the waves and spray rolled up 30 feet high, and dashed against the rock-ribbed shore with a noise like thunder, and seemed to cause the very atmosphere to tremble. Never before had we witnessed such a wild and fierce struggle of the waves, though for many years accustomed to the ocean. One whole day and two nights we could not leave our tent, which was securely anchored to the side of a hill, because of the high wind and drifting sand
, with its roaming wild dogs and extremes of weather, was "home" to
for 18 years. And up and down
. But nothing was done to ascertain the nature of her fate. Time rolled on, and all believed she was dead.
of his own, made a trip to
for seagull eggs which were in great demand at the time. Arriving in the early morning he and

walked up the beach and found at a short distance from the shore footprints made by a human being. They were well-defined in the mud and quite small, evidently those of a woman. Further on they found circular huts, but these showed no signs of recent use. Outside these huts, however, they found stakes containing seal blubber which

conjectured to be about 4 months old. They had seen enough to convince them of the existence of some human being and intended to make a further search -- but the invariable gale arose and they were hard pressed to get
heard of the discovery he requested
to make a thorough search for
In the winter of the same year
fitted out for another trip to

, they found in the crotch of one of the bushes a basket woven of grass. It contained a gown made of the skin of the shag (cormorant), bone needles, a sinew rope and some fishhooks made of abalone shells. After examining the things they were on the point of replacing them when it occurred to
to scatter them about. If they found them replaced when they made their next visit, they would know for certain that
still lived. But a strong wind caused the swells to run so heavy that they had to sail to
. This time he had in his crew, besides
was not very sanguine about finding
, as he had come to the conclusion that the wild dogs had devoured her. After landing on
walked a good 6 miles up the beach to the head of
where they again discovered footprints. But the approach of evening forced them to return to
. In the simple account each of
gives of the events of this first day, there is nothing of the romantic quality of Mrs. Hardacre's narrative:
strolling down the beach enjoying their pipes; the limpid
night; the moon burning with an amber hue; the fragrance in the air. "
were about to retrace their steps,
stopped, looked quickly about him, then stooped and closely examined something on the ground. In the weird moonlight, plainly outlined on the lonely shore, was the print of a slender, naked foot. '
" -- Well, at least it sounds good.
Immediately after breakfast the following morning,
, began what was to be a memorable day. "
struck out over the sandy stretch where on the previous trip the grass basket with its gown and bone needles had been found. This they found with its contents carefully replaced. In the meantime
went off in another direction. When
grew tired and sat down to rest,
(Dittmann) continued his search around the head of
and up to the top of the ridge. From this vantage point
espied something far up ahead which he thought to be a crow -- and whatever it was it definitely moved. Approaching cautiously he discovered it to be
She was facing in the opposite direction and hence did not notice him, though she apparently looked up every now and then and shaded her eyes to watch

searching down on the sandy stretch. Her dress was a garment made of skins of the shag with the feathers pointing downwards. This sleeveless gown reached to her ankles, and was girded about the middle with a rope of sinew.
by holding aloft his hat on the end of a ramrod and waving it up and down.
watched them without being in the least disturbed or excited. While
circled about in order to prevent her escape,

stepped from the screening bushes and confronted her. He expected her to register alarm; to his surprise she bowed and smiled and seemed delighted to see him, apparently no more affected than if his visit were an everyday occurrence. She kept up a rapid conversation with herself, nobody understanding a word. As each of
approached she bowed, smiled and spoke to them.
who came to the ridge and received the same cordial welcome. Immediately she set about to prove herself quite a hostess by roasting some roots which she passed around to

gives a description of her as she appeared when discovered. She was heavy-set and of medium height, appearing to be about fifty years old. Her features were pleasing and were made more so by the fact that she was continually smiling. Though she was missing no teeth they were badly worn, due probably to her constant diet of seal blubber. Her hair was a dull brown color and was thickly matted.
the Fathers at the Santa Barbara Mission
attempted to convey to her the idea of departure; but she did not seem to understand what he meant.

, being a little better at pantomine, went through the motions of packing her things, placing them on his back and heading for the beach. At the same time he said "vamos." This she apparently understood, for
writes: "upon hearing it her face brightened up and she set to work with alacrity to get ready."
had their hands full because she took practically everything she had. When they reached the beach she climbed in to
without any hesitation, and then crawled forward on hands and knees to the bow where she knelt holding on to both sides. That afternoon
set to work on a petticoat for
. The sum total of his labor ended in an ensemble which consisted of a skirt, a man's shirt, a black necktie and an old coat. This was her apparel during the following month she remained with

had seen hard times was evidenced from fact that she saved every bone and scrap. From this private store gradually accumulated she would from time to time extricate the bones, sucking them over and over.
Though her 18 years of solitude had been trying she still retained a heart that was young, and gay, and could find amusement in the smallest things. A baby otter
killed and stuffed afforded her great amusement.
ran into one of those invariable storms.
When the wind began to blow strongly
gave evidence of her belief in a supernatural power. She made signs to
that she would quiet the wind and waves. She knelt for sometime, apparently engaged in prayer. When the wind finally abated she looked at
and smiled, as if to say that her prayers had really brought about the quieting of the storm.
and the news of her rescue spread rapidly. Half
that night. During the following weeks
at the endeavored to find Indians from different parts of
who might understand her; but to no avail.
Inspite of this handicap of language she was always gay and happy, taking special delight in playing with
. Naturally many curious visitors came to see her, and for these she would dance and occasionally don her bird-skin dress.
In a short time she became so proficient in sign language that
were able to understand many things she wanted to say. She made known the fact that on that day 18 years beforeshe had not found
for many days without so much as a bit of food.
claimed that her gentility indicated she was not
by shipwreck. Though there can be no doubt of her noble and distinctive traits, any account which makes her out as anything but an Indian is purely fictitious.
The happy days she spent in
were to terminate even more abruptly than had her
exile. Just seven weeks after her arrival
was called to the bedside of
. He gave her conditional baptism and the Christian name of
. Her mortal remains were finally laid to rest in the
on the 19th of October, in the year 1853.
the lone woman of San Nicolas Island
. This strange tale has come down to us in the written accounts of
and and Carl Dittmann, and has been a popular tradition in
When methodical historians came to write the history of
, they sought confirmation of the story in the official records of
. When no document was found attesting the alledged baptism by
, an element of doubt began to enshroud the story.

, the famed historian of the California Missions, spent a great deal of time looking for the missing entry. The fruitlessness of his search prompted him to write in his Santa Barbara Mission: "Were it not that
fame for veracity appears above suspicion, we should declare the whole story a myth, for the reason that, after a close examination of the Baptismal Registers at
the Mission and at the Parish Church of Santa Barbara
, we failed to discover any entry regarding the woman's Baptism in either
. Likewise, there is no record noted of her burial in
. . . a document or paper containing the story of
we cannot verify. We found no such account among the numerous papers in
was approached again on this same subject by
, who was editing the personal memoirs of
in a volume entitled, The Life and Adventures of George Nidever.
if there were any further light on the question of the baptism and burial of
. He said he knew of none but that he would reexamine the Mission records. In a letter from him to
, May 14, 1932, he says: 'The Baptismal Register from 1851 to 1858 has no
or anyone else . . . I believe it will never be cleared up, as there is no document extant in our archives . . .'"
On the evening of January 19, 1944, two clerics of
, were doing some research on the
incident for an article to appear in their student publication
. In the lower left hand corner opposite page 114 of the Burial Register of the Santa Barbara Presidio they found the following entry:
En 19 de Octe de 1853 di sepulta ecca en
, y como no habia qn. le entendiese su idioma fue bautizada, sub condicione pr el
; y pa qe conste lo firmo.
Fr. Je Mar de JS Gonzz
Indian woman. On the 19th of October of 1853 I gave ecclesiastical burial in
, and since there was no one who understood her language she was baptized conditionally by
; and that this may stand as true I sign it.
Father Jose Maria de Jesus Gonzalez
's opera, Natoma. But it is a far cry from the real life story of
